notes.anchor: the most frivolous tribune of the plebs Fufius brought Pompey forward to a public meeting
notes.note: Q. Fufius Calenus, tribune of the plebs in 61 BC and a partisan of Clodius. A tribune could summon a magistrate or private man to a contio (an informal public meeting, not a voting assembly) and put questions to him before the people.

notes.anchor: The matter was being held in the Circus Flaminius
notes.note: The Circus Flaminius lay in the Campus Martius outside the pomerium (the sacred boundary), so meetings there could include Pompey, who as a triumphator-in-waiting could not enter the city proper without forfeiting his triumph.

notes.anchor: whether it pleased him that the judges should be selected by the praetor, the same praetor presiding
notes.note: The senate's bill against the Bona Dea sacrilege proposed an extraordinary court whose jurors would be hand-picked by the presiding praetor rather than drawn by lot, making conviction of Clodius far more likely; this is the point Pompey is pressed to endorse.

notes.anchor: of which you are the Aristarchus
notes.note: Aristarchus of Samothrace was the great Alexandrian critic and editor of Homer, a byword for exacting literary judgement; Cicero flatters Atticus as the discerning critic of his speeches.

notes.anchor: of the concord of the equestrians
notes.note: The concordia ordinum, the harmony between the senate and the equestrian order (the equites, the wealthy non-senatorial class from which jurors and the tax-farming publicani were drawn), was the watchword of Cicero's political programme after his consulship.
The Areopagus was the venerable homicide council of Athens, a symbol of grave and incorruptible authority; Cicero pays the senate the highest compliment by the comparison.

notes.anchor: with little daughter Curio as their leader
notes.note: "Curio's little daughter" (filiola Curionis) is a sneer at the young C. Scribonius Curio, son of the consul of 76, mocked for his effeminacy; he led the bands of fashionable youths supporting Clodius. The plain "Curio" lower in the section who proposed no decree is by contrast his father, the consular.

notes.anchor: Clodius's gangs had occupied the bridges; the voting tablets were being distributed in such a way that none ``As you propose'' was given
notes.note: Voters crossed narrow gangways (pontes) to cast ballots; controlling them let Clodius's men hand out only "ANTIQUO" (reject) tablets and withhold the "VTI ROGAS" ("as you propose," i.e. yes) tablets, rigging the vote against the bill.

notes.anchor: about fifteen men assented to Curio when he made no senate's decree; on the other side there were easily four hundred
notes.note: A senatorial vote was taken by physical division (discessio): senators walked to the side of the proposal they backed. The lopsided 400-to-15 split shows Clodius's faction was crushed in the senate even as he obstructed the popular vote.

notes.anchor: insulted Lucullus, Hortensius, Gaius Piso, Messalla the consul
notes.note: "Gaius Piso" here is C. Calpurnius Piso, consul 67 (the pacificator of the Allobroges), one of the leading consulars Clodius abused; he is distinct from the sitting consul M. Pupius Piso.
Cornutus, a tribune of the plebs in 61, affected Cato's rigid sternness without his substance, earning Cicero's nickname "pseudo-Cato"; the Cato meant is M. Porcius Cato Uticensis.
The "Pacilian house" (Paciliana domus) was a property formerly belonging to a man named Pacilius, on the open market; Quintus is selling his Tusculan villa to finance the purchase.
The Ides fell on the 13th of February (and of most months); this dates the letter to 13 February 61 BC.
